    -- We need to calculate the probability that the product of n digits (from 1 to 9) is divisible by 10.
    -- This means the product must contain both factors 2 and 5.
    -- We will use the inclusion-exclusion principle to find this probability.
    -- First, calculate the probability that the product is not divisible by 2 or not divisible by 5.
    -- Then subtract these probabilities from 1 to get the desired result.
    -- The probability that the product is not divisible by 2 is (5/9)^n since there are 5 odd digits (1, 3, 5, 7, 9).
    -- The probability that the product is not divisible by 5 is (8/9)^n since there are 8 digits not equal to 5.
    -- The probability that the product is neither divisible by 2 nor by 5 is (4/9)^n since there are 4 digits (1, 3, 7, 9) that are neither even nor 5.
    -- Therefore, the probability that the product is divisible by 10 is 1 - (8/9)^n - (5/9)^n + (4/9)^n.
    simp [unifDistN, good_seqs, is_good_seq, to_nat_digit, solution]
    <;>
    (try norm_num) <;>
    (try ring_nf) <;>
    (try field_simp) <;>
    (try norm_cast) <;>
